macro_segments.title: Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel dispute on Miun: presence and beis din requirements

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the dispute: whether miun requires presence and beis din
The camel driver counter-example from Beis Hillel and Beis Shammai's response
Explanation of 'midah kefusha' and the principle of proportional response
Gemara's resolution: two violations (trei kuteiri) in the case of improper dealing with property

macro_segments.title: Mishna on Chalitza and Miun: three witnesses requirement and debate over consensus

micro_segments.title: Reference to Sanhedrin: the three-witness requirement for miun according to Beis Shammai
Abuye's interpretation: Beis Hillel requires three even without mumchin
Minority views: Rabbi Yose and Rabbi Elazar permit miun with only two

macro_segments.title: Miun eligibility: determining who can perform miun and requirements for validity

micro_segments.title: Beis Shammai vs. Beis Hillel on multiple miun: whether a minor can do miun twice
Shmuel's requirement: reaffirmation of miun upon reaching adulthood
Definition of a minor eligible for miun: marriage with knowledge and consent
Rabbi Chanina ben Antignos: minor must be able to watch (protect) kiddushin
Rabbi Eliezer's radical position: miun of non-paternal marriages creates no real kiddushin

macro_segments.title: Practical expressions of miun: the documentary form and requirements of utterance

micro_segments.title: Original lengthy language for miun documents and why it was shortened to prevent confusion with get

macro_segments.title: Practical expressions of miun: the documentary form and requirements of utterance (continued)

micro_segments.title: Standard formula for miun: simple statement of refusal in presence of witnesses

macro_segments.title: Scope and contexts of valid miun: when utterance counts despite unusual circumstances

micro_segments.title: Miun is valid even in his absence and without formal beis din (per Rabbi Yehuda)
Miun in the wedding carriage: valid even mid-ceremony before entering the new home
Miun while serving guests: valid even while functioning as a wife in husband's household
Miun to the storekeeper: valid even when frustration or complaint seems present
Gemara's principle: 'No greater miun than this' - emphasis on empowerment and will

macro_segments.title: Miun via alternative actions: marriage or acceptance of kiddushin to another as miun substitute

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda ben Beteira: marriage or kiddushin to another guy counts as miun from the first
Gemara's questions: does kiddushin alone suffice or only nisuin as miun
Ruling: even kiddushin from another man constitutes miun, even if first marriage was nisuin
Case of Avdin's daughters-in-law: verbal rejection to potential rival as miun

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's conceptualization: kiddushei ketanah without father as non-marriage concubinage

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's consistency principle: if minor's act binds, it cannot be valid kiddushin
Implications of non-kiddushin status: no rights to earnings, vows, inheritance, or tumah
Rabbi Yehoshua's opposite extreme: full wife status with all marital rights despite miun eligibility
Rabbi's evaluation: Rabbi Eliezer more consistent; Rabbi Yehoshua's distinction problematic

macro_segments.title: Miun vs. Get: status redefinition and implications for remarriage (machzir gerushato)

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's reading: refusal of second suitor signals consent to first marriage
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: miun as indicator of actual consent vs. acceptance of marriage
Case scenario: if miun follows get, woman is not a gerusha and can remarry the first man
Contrary scenario: if get follows miun, woman is gerusha and forbidden to first husband

macro_segments.title: Complex cases: when miun from one man cannot redeem status from a different man's get

micro_segments.title: Three-guy scenario: order of miun and get determines status with each partner separately
Rule formulated: only same-partner miun can override same-partner get, not different-partner miun
Gemara's concern: allowing foreign miun to override get gives first husband leverage to ruin second marriage
Counter-case resolution: if miun came first, he already tried and failed, reducing concern of future leverage
